text.title: Spatial Accessibility: Agentic Audit Workflow and Platform Gotchas
text.summary: Agent workflow for spatial accessibility auditing: subagent roles, JSON interaction schema, and platform-specific gotchas that LLMs commonly miss in XR design reviews.

section.title: Agentic Workflow
section.p: Use three subagents: one maps every interaction to the alternative-input matrix (gaze, voice, hand, controller, switch); a second audits comfort settings and seated-mode coverage; a third generates audio descriptions and caption placement specs. Validate all outputs on hardware with real users — synthetic testing misses motion sickness and field-of-view blind spots.

rule.statement: Require hardware testing with at least five users including one low-vision, one motor-impaired, and one motion-sensitive tester before claiming spatial accessibility compliance.
rule.rationale: No automated tool or agent can substitute for real user validation in spatial contexts. Comfort settings that "feel fine" to designers routinely fail real users with vestibular disorders.

example.note: Interaction audit prompt: "For each spatial interaction, return JSON: { name, primary_input, alternatives[], requires_motion: bool, comfort_options[], seated_compatible: bool, blockers[] }. Required alternatives: at least one of {gaze+dwell, voice, single-hand, controller, switch}. If requires_motion, comfort_options must include vignette, teleport, snap-turn, FOV-reduce, or seated."

antipattern.description: LLMs defaulting to controller-only assumptions without enumerating alternatives explicitly.
antipattern.reason: Controllers are unavailable or unusable in many spatial contexts. Force the agent to enumerate all modality alternatives for each interaction rather than assuming a default input path.

antipattern.description: Agent recommending head-locked HUD as a standard pattern from older XR literature.
antipattern.reason: Apple HIG visionOS forbids head-locked UI; Meta guidelines strongly discourage it for non-telemetry use. Current guidance is the opposite of legacy XR documentation that LLMs were trained on.

antipattern.description: Declaring spatial accessibility compliance without hardware testing on real assistive users.
antipattern.reason: Synthetic XR a11y testing tools are immature. Motion sickness is individually variable and cannot be detected by static analysis. Only real-user hardware testing produces valid compliance evidence.

antipattern.description: Agent recommending head-locked captions universally without considering immersion context.
antipattern.reason: Caption placement reasoning is poor in current LLMs. Head-locked captions are readable but break immersion; world-locked captions are immersive but unreadable in motion. Heuristics plus human review are required.

list.item: WCAG does not fully cover spatial UI; W3C XAUR (XR Accessibility User Requirements) is informative, not normative — checklists must be authored by hand.
VoiceOver on visionOS is partial; hand-authored audio descriptions are still required for blind users.
Interaction zones must scale to accommodate hand size, dominance, and tremor — treat these as first-class input variables.
